summary: Gruber systematically rebuts Nilay Patel's argument that removing the iPhone headphone jack is 'user-hostile and stupid' by drawing parallels to Apple's history of removing legacy ports, starting with the iMac's floppy drive in 1998. He acknowledges legitimate concerns — particularly around MFi licensing restrictions and incompatibility with MacBooks — but argues that the analog headphone jack must eventually go, and Apple has historically been good at timing these transitions. He dismisses the thinness argument by noting the iPod Touch and Nano are thinner yet retain the jack, suggesting depth rather than thickness is the real constraint. His core position is that Apple's essence is making design decisions 'no one asked for,' and in five years the headphone jack will seem as antiquated as VGA ports.
key_points: The real concern isn't DRM on audio playback but Apple's MFi Program restricting which manufacturers can make Lightning accessories
Device thinness isn't the motivation — the iPod Touch is thinner than any iPhone and has a headphone jack; the jack costs depth, not thickness
Apple has never prioritized port compatibility with competitors, so Android headphone incompatibility is irrelevant to their calculus
The meaningful incompatibility problem is between Apple's own devices — Lightning earbuds won't work on MacBooks without a dongle
Apple's entire history is removing established ports before anyone asks them to — floppy drives, ADB, 30-pin connectors, VGA, serial ports
money_quotes: Should the analog headphone jack remain on our devices forever? If you think so, you can stop reading.
Historically, Apple has proven to be very good at timing the removal of established legacy ports.
It's not enforcement of DRM on audio playback. It's enforcement of the MFi Program for certifying hardware that uses the Lightning port.
Whatever the merits of this decision, it's not about device thinness.
"Enabling, open, and democratizing" have never been high on Apple's list of priorities for external ports. They're on the list, to be sure. Just not high on the list.
If Apple gave two shits about port compatibility with Android, iPhones would have Micro-USB ports.
This is how it goes. If it weren't for Apple we'd probably still be using computers with VGA and serial ports.
The essence of Apple is that they make design decisions "no one asked for".
key_insight: Removing the headphone jack follows Apple's long pattern of killing legacy ports before anyone is ready, and history suggests the outcry will fade as the industry adapts.
